summary:
The making of bread — the grinding of grain into flour and the baking or roasting of the resulting dough — appears to predate fully domesticated cereal agriculture by several thousand years. Charred remains from the site of Shubayqa 1 in northeastern Jordan, dated to approximately 12,400–11,600 BCE and associated with [[event:4|Natufian]] hunter-gatherers, have been identified as the products of bread-making, predating the establishment of farming in the Levant by at least 4,000 years.

This discovery from the Natufian period implies that bread-making using wild cereal grains was a significant food preparation technology among the last hunter-gatherers of the Levant, possibly before systematic cultivation began. The labor-intensive process of collecting, processing, and baking wild grain suggests that bread held special social or ritual significance beyond mere sustenance — it may have been produced for feasting, ceremony, or as a prestige food before becoming a dietary staple.

The earliest bread was unleavened — flatbreads made from ground wild cereals and cooked on hot stones or in simple hearths. With the transition to settled agriculture following the [[event:1304|Pre-Pottery Neolithic A]], grain processing and bread-making became central to the food economy, with saddle querns, mortars, and pestles ubiquitous at agricultural sites. Leavened (risen) bread using fermented dough would follow later, once communities had the settled conditions necessary to maintain sourdough cultures.
